did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an ensemble of deep neural networks (CNNs: AlexNet, VGGNet, GoogLeNet) for the classification of skin lesions (nevus, malignant melanoma, seborrheic keratosis) using the ISBI 2017 challenge dataset. The method involves fusing these CNN architectures via a joint fully connected layer and weighted outputs, trained using backpropagation and transfer learning.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel deep learning-based approach (an ensemble of CNNs: AlexNet, VGGNet, GoogLeNet) for skin lesion classification. It details a specific methodology (fusion via joint fully connected layer), describes the dataset used (ISBI 2017 challenge), and reports quantitative results (accuracy and AUC) comparing the proposed method against individual member CNNs. The structure includes Abstract, Introduction, Methodology, and Performance Evaluation sections typical of original research.

title: Classification of skin lesions using an ensemble of deep neural networks
journal: IEEE International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
brief_summary: This study proposes a deep learning-based approach for the classification of skin lesions, specifically distinguishing between nevus, malignant melanoma, and seborrheic keratosis. The authors construct an ensemble of three state-of-the-art convolutional neural networks (AlexNet, VGGNet, and GoogLeNet) by fusing their features through a joint fully connected layer with weighted outputs. The model was trained using transfer learning and data augmentation on the ISBI 2017 challenge dataset, which included 2,000 training images. Performance was evaluated using accuracy and area-under-the-curve (AUC) metrics via a one-vs-all binary classification strategy. The experimental results demonstrated that the proposed ensemble method outperformed all individual member CNNs and pairwise ensembles, achieving competitive classification accuracy.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three classes in the ISBI 2017 challenge dataset used for classification. Training set count: 1,372 (increased to 8,200 with augmentation). Test set count: 393.
One of the three classes in the ISBI 2017 challenge dataset used for classification. Training set count: 374 (increased to 4,600 with augmentation). Test set count: 90.
One of the three classes in the ISBI 2017 challenge dataset used for classification. Training set count: 254 (increased to 1,500 with augmentation). Test set count: 117.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (nevus, malignant melanoma, seborrheic keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs a three-class classification task to recognize nevus (healthy lesion), malignant melanoma (cancerous lesion), and seborrheic keratosis (age-related, non-cancerous lesion). The model predicts discrete class labels using a softmax layer. Performance was evaluated using accuracy and AUC via a one-vs-all binary classification strategy.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset released during the IEEE International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI) 2017 challenge dedicated to skin cancer recognition. It contains dermatoscopic images of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training the ensemble of CNNs (AlexNet, VGGNet, GoogLeNet) with data augmentation (cropping, horizontal flipping, rotating) increasing the effective training set to 14,300 images.
use.testing: Used for independent performance evaluation of the proposed ensemble and member CNNs using accuracy and AUC metrics.
use.other: The pre-trained weights for the member CNNs were originally obtained from the ImageNet dataset (1.28 million natural images), but the specific ImageNet dataset is not the primary dataset for the skin lesion task.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To convert the three-class classification task into binary problems for the calculation of accuracy and AUC metrics.
To evaluate classification performance at the 0.5 confidence level on the ROC curve.
To provide a detailed description of classification performance across different thresholds.
To optimize the parameters of the neural network architectures during training.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using accuracy (ACC) and area-under-the-curve (AUC) metrics without statistical significance testing.

metric: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC)
ISBI 2017 Challenge Dataset (Test Set).evaluation_setting: One-vs-all binary classification for three classes: Nevus (CN), Malignant Melanoma (CM), Seborrheic Keratosis (CSK). Average (AVG) performance across the three scenarios is also reported.
ISBI 2017 Challenge Dataset (Test Set).notes: The proposed ensemble-based system (GoogLeNet & AlexNet & VGGNet) outperformed all individual member CNNs and pairwise ensembles in both average accuracy and average AUC. The paper did not report statistical significance tests (p-values) for these comparisons.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of AlexNet, VGGNet, and GoogLeNet
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained weights from ImageNet (1.28 million natural images)
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (standard for member CNNs)

member_models: AlexNet: 5 convolutional layers, max-pooling, 3 fully-connected layers, softmax
VGGNet (VGG16): 13 convolutional layers (3x3 filters), 5 max-pooling layers (2x2 mask, stride 2), 3 fully-connected layers
GoogLeNet: 22 convolutional layers including 9 Inception modules (kernels 5x5, 3x3, 1x1 and 3x3 pooling)
architecture_details.fusion_method: Removal of original final fully-connected and classification layers; replacement with 3-dimensional fully-connected layers; aggregation via joint fully connected layer (fc_AVG) with weighted outputs (matrices A1, A2, A3 initialized as diagonal matrices with 1/3 on diagonal)
architecture_details.final_layers: Softmax/classification layer following the aggregation layer

training_details.loss_function: Mean Squared Error (MSE) between normalized aggregated output and ground truth labels
training_details.data_augmentation: Cropping, horizontal flipping, rotating (training set increased from 2,000 to 14,300 images)
training_details.hardware: NVIDIA TITAN X GPU (7 TFlops, 336.5 GB/s memory bandwidth, 3,072 CUDA cores, 12 GB memory)

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: cropping, horizontal flipping, and rotating images to increase the training set size from 2,000 to 14,300 images.
Transfer learning: using pre-trained weights and biases from ImageNet for the member CNNs (AlexNet, VGGNet, GoogLeNet).
Label normalization: ground truth labels normalized to vectors (e.g., (1, 0, 0), (0, 1, 0), (0, 0, 1)) for the three classes.
One-vs-all binary classification strategy: converting the three-class task into binary problems for evaluation metrics calculation.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training to increase dataset size; specific ranges or probabilities not reported.
Applied during training to increase dataset size; specific probabilities not reported.
Applied during training to increase dataset size; specific angles or probabilities not reported.

conclusions: The proposed ensemble-based approach, which fuses AlexNet, VGGNet, and GoogLeNet via a joint fully connected layer with weighted outputs, outperformed all individual member CNNs and pairwise ensembles in classifying skin lesions (nevus, malignant melanoma, and seborrheic keratosis).
The ensemble achieved an average accuracy of 0.838 and an average AUC of 0.848 on the ISBI 2017 challenge test set, demonstrating competitive performance in the field of skin cancer recognition.
The framework allows for the automatic adjustment of weights for the member CNN outputs via backpropagation, eliminating the need for manual tuning.
The modular architecture of the ensemble allows for the inclusion of additional CNN members in the future to potentially improve performance.
Regularization issues regarding the weighting parameters used in the aggregation of ensemble members to avoid overfitting may be addressed by adding corresponding terms to the loss function.
Increasing the volume of the training dataset is expected to raise the performance of the combined architecture, given its complexity.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISBI 2017 challenge dataset for both training and testing, with no external validation on independent clinical datasets, limiting generalizability assessment.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance in the training set (Nevus: 1,372; Melanoma: 374; Seborrheic Keratosis: 254), which may bias the model towards the majority class despite data augmentation.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to validate the performance improvements of the ensemble over individual CNNs or pairwise ensembles.
The authors acknowledge potential regularization issues regarding the weighting parameters used in the aggregation layer to prevent overfitting, suggesting this as a limitation.

The model was trained on a relatively small dataset (2,000 images), augmented to 14,300, which may limit the robustness of the deep learning approach compared to models trained on larger, multi-center datasets.

The hardware dependency is high, requiring an NVIDIA TITAN X GPU for training, with significant training times (e.g., 287.5 hours for the ensemble), posing deployment concerns for resource-constrained environments.
